coredata.dc:title: Gold nanoclusters and amino-modified mesoporous silica-encapsulated carbon dot fluorescence nanosensors combined with LightGBM algorithm for ultra-fast detection of Co2+
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kang, Chenyue
Li, Yueying
Wang, Xuran
Zhang, Yan
Zhang, Caihong
Zhou, Ying
Shuang, Shaomin
Zhang, Guomei
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we develop a novel ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for the detection excess Co2+ in soil. This fluorescent nanosensor is fabricated using mesoporous silica-encapsulated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs@mSiO2) and gold nanoclusters stabilized by bovine serum albumin (BSA-AuNCs). The green fluorescence of the carbon dots within the mesoporous silica spheres (mSiO2) serves as an internal reference signal. The red fluoresence BSA-AuNCs are covalently connected onto the surface of amino-functionalized nanospheres (N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2), providing the response signal. This nanosensor has dual emission peaks at 520 nm and 650 nm under excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The addition of Co2+ to this nanosensor causes the fluorescence quenching at 650 nm while the green fluorescence at 520 nm remains unchanged, resulting in fluorescence color change from yellow to green. The developed ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor exhibits excellent selectivity to Co2+ with a range of 2.00–200.00 μM and a detection limit as low as 0.74 μM. In addition, a Co2+ prediction model is developed using the Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) algorithm. The entire detection process is within 10 s and the model prediction value is as high as 0.991 on average. This shows that the proposed fluorescent nanosensor, optimized with the LightGBM algorithm, provides an efficient, environmentally friendly and potentially practical solution for Co2+ detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs
Ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor
Amidation reaction
LightGBM algorithm
Co2+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: MTT experiments and cell imaging of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs fluorescent nanosensor
Fluorescence detection of Co2+, selectivity of the developed ratiometric fluoresent nanosensor
Structural Characterization of BSA-AuNCs, N-CDs and N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs
Choice of materials, optimization and characterization
LightGBM training of the fluorescent nanosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images、HRTEM images of BSA-AuNCs (a) and N-CDs (b). The high-resolution TEM image (c)、TEM image (d). SEM image (e) and EDX energy spectra (f) of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs nanocomposite.

(a) IR spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs@mSiO2, N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2 and N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs. XPS of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs: (b) survey scan, (c) high resolution Si2p spectrum, (d) high resolution O1s spectrum, (e) high resolution Au4f spectrum, (f) high resolution N1s spectrum.

(a) The hydrodynamic diameter of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs. (b) Zeta potentials. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra. (d) Excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs. (e) TGA curves of mSiO2 and N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs from 0 ℃ to 800 ℃ (10 ℃/min). N2 adsorption/desorption curve and BJH pore size distribution of mSiO2 (f)、N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2 (g) and N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs (h), respectively.
Images of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs collected under fluorescence colocalisation imaging in HeLa cells; (a) Bright field; (b) Red Channel; (c) Green Channel; (d) Merge channel.

(a) Emission spectra of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs with various concentrations of Co2+ (0.00–200.00 μM). The upper inset displays the images of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs with various concentrations of Co2+ under UV light. The concentration of Co2+ increases from left (0.00) to right (200.00 μM), The lower inset displays the ratiometric F650 /F520 calibration curve of Co2+ (2.00–200.00 μM) using N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs. (b) F650 /F520 of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs nanocomposite in the presence of various potential interferents and Co2+. (c) Schematic diagram of the LightGBM algorithm for the detection of Co2+. (d) LightGBM model construction.
The determination of Co2+ in real soil samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Gold nanoclusters and amino-modified mesoporous silica-encapsulated carbon dot fluorescence nanosensors combined with LightGBM algorithm for ultra-fast detection of Co2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Key Laboratory of Human-Machine Hybrid Augmented Intelligence, National Engineering Research Center for Visual Information and Applications, Institute of Artificial Intelligence and Robotics, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Key Laboratory of Human-Machine Hybrid Augmented Intelligence
National Engineering Research Center for Visual Information and Applications
Institute of Artificial Intelligence and Robotics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Key Laboratory of Human-Machine Hybrid Augmented Intelligence, National Engineering Research Center for Visual Information and Applications, Institute of Artificial Intelligence and Robotics, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xi’an Aerospace Composites Research Institute, Xi’an 710025, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xi’an Aerospace Composites Research Institute, Xi’an 710025, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we develop a novel ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for the detection excess Co2+ in soil. This fluorescent nanosensor is fabricated using mesoporous silica-encapsulated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs@mSiO2) and gold nanoclusters stabilized by bovine serum albumin (BSA-AuNCs). The green fluorescence of the carbon dots within the mesoporous silica spheres (mSiO2) serves as an internal reference signal. The red fluoresence BSA-AuNCs are covalently connected onto the surface of amino-functionalized nanospheres (N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2), providing the response signal. This nanosensor has dual emission peaks at 520 nm and 650 nm under excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The addition of Co2+ to this nanosensor causes the fluorescence quenching at 650 nm while the green fluorescence at 520 nm remains unchanged, resulting in fluorescence color change from yellow to green. The developed ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor exhibits excellent selectivity to Co2+ with a range of 2.00–200.00 μM and a detection limit as low as 0.74 μM. In addition, a Co2+ prediction model is developed using the Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) algorithm. The entire detection process is within 10 s and the model prediction value is as high as 0.991 on average. This shows that the proposed fluorescent nanosensor, optimized with the LightGBM algorithm, provides an efficient, environmentally friendly and potentially practical solution for Co2+ detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt (Co) is prevalent in nature and serves as an essential trace element in the human body [1]. However, elevated concentrations of cobalt (II) can lead to serious health issues, including low blood pressure, bone defects, erythrocytosis, skin irritation, and gene mutations in living cells [2]. Nowadays, with the development of the manufacturing industry cobalt is widely used in the production of alloys, battery materials, paints and diamonds, Co pollution is becoming more and more serious. Excessive Co2+ ions in the environment not only greatly inhibit the metabolism and growth of microorganisms but can also be absorbed by humans through agricultural crops and the food chain [3]. Consequently, developing an efficient method for detecting of Co2+ in soil is of great importance. Current detection methods for Co2+ ions include atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [4–6] among others. But, some of these methods are complicated, costly, and involve lengthy operational cycles. Other techniques may be simple and inexpensive but suffer from low sensitivity and accuracy. Thus, it is significant to explore a method that combines a short detection cycle with high sensitivity, good selectivity, and cost-effectiveness for detecting Co2+ pollution in the environment.

Fluorescence (FL) technology is widely employed as a sensitive detection strategy in the biomedical and biosensing fields [7,8]. In recent years, novel nanomaterials have demonstrated significant potential for fluorescence detection, particularly carbon dots (CDs) [9] and metal nanoclusters (MNCs) [10], which have garnered considerable attention due to their excellent biocompatibility [11,12], superior fluorescence properties [13], and stability [14]. However, despite the advantages of these fluorescent probes regarding analytical performance, they still encounter inherent limitations [15]. For instance, single-emission fluorescent probes are often constrained by environmental conditions [16], low quantum yields [17], photobleaching [18], and sample autofluorescence interferences [19], which can lead to false-positive signals and subsequently affect the sensitivity and accuracy of assays [20]. To address these challenges, dual-emission fluorescent probe technology has been developed [21]. By simultaneously monitoring fluorescent signals at two distinct wavelengths, dual-emission technology can significantly enhance the sensitivity and specificity of detection [22]. This multi-emission mode not only mitigates the influence of interfering signals but also provides additional analytical information to identify target molecules more accurately, thereby overcoming the limitations of traditional single-emission fluorescent probes in complex samples [23–25]. Furthermore, to further combat the photobleaching effect and environmental interference associated with quantum dots, the application of core–shell structures [26], particularly mesoporous silica (mSiO2) [27,28], has emerged as an effective solution. The mesoporous silica shell layer effectively protects the fluorescent material, reduces photobleaching, and enhances the dispersion of the fluorescent material [29]. These properties substantially improve the stability and quantum yield of fluorescent materials. Consequently, mSiO2 not only enhances the performance of fluorescent probes but also plays a crucial role in improving detection sensitivity [30].

To address the limitations of traditional fluorescence sensing methods, multimodal fluorescence sensing technology has emerged as an innovative solution. This technology enhances detection accuracy by integrating fluorescence data with colorimetric changes [31]. By simultaneously acquiring information from multiple signal sources, it significantly improves the recognition of target molecules while minimizing the impact of environmental interference on detection results. Currently, a commonly employed method involves recording red, green, and blue (RGB) data in conjunction with the detected concentration [32]. However, this technology depends on specialized operators, intricate operational methods, and lengthy detection cycles when handling large volumes of data. To address these challenges, we propose a novel method that integrates machine learning algorithms with fluorescence technology, enabling rapid, accurate, and time-efficient detection. Unsupervised learning discovers hidden patterns from unlabeled data, including methods like clustering, dimensionality reduction, association rule learning, etc [33]. While supervised learning perform predicting or classifying tasks using paired training data, which consists of feature vectors and corresponding label values. Due to the corresponding concentration and RGB values in the dataset of this experiment, it is suitable to apply supervised machine learning methods for concentration prediction. Among the supervised machine learning algorithms, LightGBM, an integrated enhancement model developed by Microsoft, excels in effectively addressing these challenges [34–37].

Fluorescence experiments were performed on F-4500 spectrophotometer(Tokyo, Japan). A Hitachi UV-2910 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) was used to record UV–visible absorption spectra. XPS was performed on a Hitachi Kratos AxisUltradld (Tokyo, Japan). A Malvern ZEN3690 Zetasizer (Westborough, MA, US) was used to measure zetapotentials and hydrodynamic diameter distribution. A JEM-2100F TEM (Tokyo, Japan) were used for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). A JSM-7001F SEM (Tokyo, Japan) was used for scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Infrared measurements were taken in Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 (Waltham, MA, US). The thermal stability of the samples was analyzed by a thermogravimetric analyzer (Rigaku TG, Tokyo) under nitrogen atmosphere. Cell imaging experiments were taken by LS-880 laser scanning confocal microscope. LightGBM was from Pycharm programming environment, python 3.8.3 (Windows 10 system, processor model Intel Core i5-8250U, 1.60 GHz).

Cetyltrimethylammonium chloride (CTAC), 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), triethanolamine (TEA), bovine serum albumin(BSA), n-hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (Sulfo-NHS) and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC) were all supplied by Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·3H2O) was got from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Institute (Tianjin, China). Salicylic acid and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were got from Tianjin Bodhi Chemical Company (Tianjin, China). Cyclohexane, ethanol, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethylenediamine and anhydrous.

The mSiO2 is based on an earlier publication. It has been slightly modified. Firstly, add 6.0 g CTAC and 0.25 mL TEA to 60 mL water and stir gently at 65 °C for 1 h in a 100 mL round-bottomed flask. Then 4 mL of TEOS in cyclohexane was carefully added to the solution and kept overnight at 70 °C in an oil bath with gentle magnetic stirring at 180 rpm [38]. Then, the products were collected by centrifugation and washing with ethanol for several times to remove the residual reactants. Finally, the mSiO2 nanospheres were obtained after the calcination at 550 ℃ for 5 h in air to remove the template.

0.3 g of salicylic acid and 200 µL of EDC were dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon beaker and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The N-CDs solution was then lyophilized and stored in a refrigerator for future use. The purified N-CDs and mSiO2 nanospheres were dispersed in ethanol and sonicated for 10 min. Afterwards, the suspension was stirred at room temperature for 5 h. Then the suspension was centrifuged to precipitate the solid N-CDs@mSiO2 which was washed with water. Finally, the residual water was removed in a vacuum drying oven to obtain N-CDs@mSiO2.

0.3 g dry N-CDs@mSiO2 was added to 20 mL ethanol and sonicated to make sure it dispersed evenly in ethanol. Then, 200 μL APTES was added to N-CDs@mSiO2 in ethanol and stirred, followed by refluxing at 70 ℃ for 6 h. Finally, washed and dried to obtain amino-modified N-CDs@mSiO2 (N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2).

According to a previous method. An aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (5 mL, 10 mM) was added to a vigorously stirred solution of BSA (5 mL, 50 mg/mL). NaOH solution (500 μL, 1 M) was then introduced, and incubated at 60 °C for 6 h [39]. Finally, purified BSA-AuNCs solution was lyophilized to obtain the yellow BSA-AuNCs solid, which was stored at refrigerator for use.

Initially, 200 μL of EDC (5 mg/mL) and 200 μL of Sulfo-NHS (5 mg/mL) were added to 500 μL of BSA-AuNCs (15 mg/mL) and stirred at 37 ℃ for 1 h to activate the carboxyl groups. Subsequently, a 500 μL solution of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2 (15 mg/mL) was incorporated and the mixture was stirred at 37 ℃ for an additional 3 h. Finally, the resultant product (N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs) was collected via centrifugation at 9,000 rpm for 30 min and washed three times with ultrapure water. The final product was then dried in a drying oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTT experiments and cell imaging of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs fluorescent nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were employed as a model to evaluate the cytotoxicity of nanomaterials using MTT assays. First, HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well culture plates and allowed to fully adhere to the walls. The old culture medium was then removed. The cells were treated with different concentrations of fluorescent nanosensors and mixed with cell culture medium for a duration of 24 h. After incubation, the culture medium was discarded, and 200 µL of fresh culture medium was added, accompanied by 20 µL of 5 mg/mL MTT reagent. Incubation continued for an additional 4–6 h. After this time, the supernatant was removed, and 150 µL of DMSO solution was added for measurement.

HeLa cells were inoculated into 35 mm culture plates overnight at a density of 1.0 × 105 cells. Add 300 μL of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs aqueous solution (1.4 mg/mL) was added to 2.0 mL of culture medium to obtain a N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs concentration of 0.3 mg/mL, followed by a 20-minute incubation. The cell culture plate was placed under a laser confocal microscope (LS-800) for optical bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Co2+, selectivity of the developed ratiometric fluoresent nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Add 0.5 mL of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs (5.0 mg/mL) to a solution containing 2.0 mL PBS (pH 7.0). Different concentrations of Co2+ were injected into the solution. The ratiometric fluorescence intensities (F650 /F440 ) of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs with Co2+ was recorded and fluorescence intensities (F650 /F440 ) curve was constructed. The selectivity was investigated with Co2+ in the presence of various interfering cations and anions such as I-, Cl-, S2-, F-, SCN-, NO3 –, CO3 2–, Li+, Na+, K+, Fe3+, Mg2+, NH4 +.

Soil samples were collected from Shanxi University. The different concentrations of Co2+ standard solutions were prepared, and the solution was thoroughly mixed with 2.0 g of the soil sample to ensure even distribution of Co2+ throughout the soil. The mixture was allowed to stand for 24 h to facilitate complete reaction and achieve equilibrium between the Co2+ ions and the soil. The samples were then dried in a low-temperature oven at 50 °C. Once dried, the soil sample was ground using a mortar and subsequently sieved to obtain a uniform fine powder. An accurate amount of the sample (1.0 g) was weighed and placed into a centrifuge tube. Appropriate acid solution was added, maintaining a soil to solution ratio of 1:15. The sample was vortexed to ensure thorough mixing. Following this, the samples were centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 20 min to separate the supernatant from the soil solid fractions. The supernatant was then filtered through a membrane filter to remove any residual particulate matter. The filtrate was diluted as necessary, and the processed sample supernatant was transferred to a clean container for storage at 4 °C for subsequent use [40]. Afterwards, 500 μL of the resulting solution were mixed with the N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs (200 μL) and incubated 20 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of the real samples were measured to detect Co2+.

The RGB values corresponding to the fluorescent nanosensors at various Co2+ concentrations were obtained using a mobile phone-based RGB extraction software under 365 nm UV light. This method involves capturing images of the fluorescent responses of the nanosensors at different Co2+ concentrations and subsequently extracting the corresponding RGB values from these images. The dataset includes a total of 600 samples, each contains a group of RGB with Co2+ concentration value. The RGB values represent the colorimetric response of the nanosensors to varying concentrations of Co2+, which are use as inputs for the concentration prediction model. The dataset was divided into training and test sets by 8:2, ensures the model is trained on a diverse dataset while retaining a separate set for unbiased evaluation. Due to its fast speed and high accuracy, LightGBM algorithm was used to build the concentration prediction model, and the concentration prediction performance was assessed using R2 (Coefficient of Determination), MSE (Mean Squared Error), and RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error). The LightGBM model's prediction performance of Co2+ concentrations was benchmarked through five runs, with an average runtime of 1.599 s. The corresponding source code of LightGBM algorithm can be found in Text S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural Characterization of BSA-AuNCs, N-CDs and N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and structure of BSA-AuNCs, N-CDs and N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs were investigated using SEM, TEM and HRTEM. Fig. 1 a and b display the TEM and HRTEM images of BSA-AuNCs and N-CDs, respectively. Gaussian fitting of the TEM-derived histograms, shown as insets in Fig. 1a and b, indicates that the particle size distributions of BSA-AuNCs and N-CDs range from 0.70 to 3.10 nm and from 1.75 to 4.25 nm, respectively. The HRTEM images show that the BSA-AuNCs have a spacing of 0.1834 nm, while the N-CDs have a spacing of 0.1720 nm. Fig. 1c and d display the HRTEM and TEM images of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs, respectively. Fig. 1c shows that mSiO2 is loaded with many N-CDs. BSA-AuNCs is bonded to the surface of the N-CDs@mSiO2. Fig. 1d shows that N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs have a clearly defined mid-radial mesoporous structure and good dispersion in water. Fig. 1e displays the SEM image of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs, revealing that these nanospheres are highly homogeneous mesoporous nanospheres with an average particle size of 150 nm. The EDX in Fig. 1f indicates that the N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs are mainly composed of four elements: Si, O, N, and Au. Specifically, Si and O are attributed to TEOS and APTES while N is attributed to N-CDs. Au is attributed to BSA-AuNCs.

IR spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs@mSiO2, N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2, and N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs were examined, as shown in Fig. 2 a. The analysis of the infrared spectrum of N-CDs consists essentially of 3289.84, 2950.56, 1625.24, 1268.42, and 735.33 cm−1, corresponding to N-H/O-H stretches, C-H stretches, C=O stretches, C-N stretches and O-disubstitution of benzene ring. IR analysis shows that the surface of N-CDs has hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl and amide functional groups, giving N-CDs good hydrophilicity. For N-CDs@mSiO2, the broad absorption band (3165.11–3590.43 cm−1) is attributed to stretching vibrations of Si-OH in SiO2 or N-H/O-H in N-CDs. Sharp absorption peaks at 1078.94 and 860.77 cm−1 correspond to Si-O-Si and O-Si-O stretching vibrations respectively. The peaks at 761.34 and 462.32 cm−1 are due to Si-O vibrations. In the IR spectrum of BSA-AuNCs, signals include O-H/N-H groups at 3359.91 cm−1, C-H groups at 2933.43 cm−1, C=O groups at 1655.78 cm−1, N-H groups at 1511.10 cm−1 and C-O groups at 1030.34 cm−1. The N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs nanocomposites contain almost all the IR absorption peaks. Additionally, new stretching vibrations of the amide C=O band and bending vibration peaks of the N-H bond were observed at 1658.00 cm−1 and 1591.89 cm−1, respectively. This confirms the successful synthesis of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs nanocomposites via the amidation reaction between the amino groups on the surface of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2 and the carboxyl groups of BSA-AuNCs. Fig. 2b presents the XPS survey scan spectrum for N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs, revealing the presence of carbon, silicon, oxygen, sulfur, nitrogen and gold. Fig. 2c depicts the XPS Si2p spectrum which has three peaks at 101.70, 102.30 and 102.90 eV, belonging to Si-C, Si-O and Si-N bonds, respectively. Fig. 2d shows the O1s spectrum, which split into 530.70 eV (O-C/O-S) and 531.80 eV (O-Si/O-H/O=C/O-SO) peaks. Fig. 2e shows the XPS Au spectrum of the nanocomposites with peaks at 83.20 eV and 87.10 eV for Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 respectively. Fig. 2f shows the deconvoluted N1s spectrum which shows two peaks: one at 398.70 eV (N-Si) and another at 399.50 eV (N-C=O/N-H). The C1s and S2p spectra of the nanocomposites are shown in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2, respectively. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum shows four peaks at 284.30 eV, 284.70 eV, 285.60 eV and 288.00 eV corresponding to Si-C-N/C-Si-C, C=C/C-Si/C-H, C-N/C-O-C/C-O-H and C=N-O/C=O bonds respectively. The S2p spectrum is deconvoluted into two peaks at 163.80 eV and 167.60 eV, belonging to C-S-C and Au-S/S-O groups, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Choice of materials, optimization and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. S3, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs were measured, which was determined to be 2.22 ns. After coating the N-CDs with mSiO2, the fluorescence lifetime increased to 3.12 ns. And the quantum yields (QYs) of N-CDs and N-CDs@mSiO2 using 0.1 M quinine sulfate as a reference were found to be 6.5 % and 8.2 % with excitation at 380 nm, respectively. These data indicate that the coated N-CDs@mSiO2 exhibit enhanced fluorescence lifetime and higher QYs, which can be attributed to the formation of Si-C and Si-N covalent bonds between mSiO2 and N-CDs during the coating process. These covalent bonds effectively restrict the rotation and vibration of the surface groups on the carbon dots, thereby reducing the non-radiative decay of electrons in the triplet state (T1) [27]. To investigate the synthesis conditions of N-CDs@mSiO2, the effects of temperatures were examined in Fig. S4. The results indicate that the synthesized N-CDs@mSiO2 exhibit the highest emission intensity at a temperature of 30 °C. To enhance the analytical performance of the developed fluorescent probes, the effects of pH on N-CDs@mSiO2 and BSA-AuNCs were investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S5. the optimal pH is 7.0.

The dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis in Fig. 3 a shows that the nanospheres of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs in ultra-pure water have a uniform hydrodynamic size distribution. The hydrodynamic average size distribution is about 163.50 nm. Fig. 3b depicts the zeta potentials of N-CDs@mSiO2, N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2, BSA-AuNCs, and N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs, respectively. N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2 shows a slightly positive surface charge (2.03 mV) in contrast to the negatively charged N-CDs@mSiO2 (−13.33 mV), which is attributed to the negatively charged N-CDs@mSiO2 coordinated with the positively charged amino groups. The zeta potentials of the BSA-AuNCs and N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs were −16.00 mV and −8.64 mV, respectively, indicating that N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2 and BSA-AuNCs aggregate to form N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs nanocomposites via electrostatic assembly. The optical properties of the nanocomposites were investigated and are shown in Fig. 3c. N-CDs exhibit two strong absorption peaks at 255 nm and 290 nm, probably due to the π → π* and n → π* electronic transitions of the surface groups. N-CDs@mSiO2 possesses a similar UV–Vis absorption bands compared to N-CDs. N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2 shows two new UV–Vis absorption bands at 220 and 240 nm, which is probably attributed to the electronic transition n → σ* orbital of the surface amino group. N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs has a similar UV–Vis absorption spectra compared to N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2; however, its UV–Vis absorption bands at 220 and 240 nm vanish probably due to the amidation reaction on the surface. As depicted in Figs. S6 and S7, emission peaks were observed for N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2 and BSA-AuNCs, with maximum peaks at 520 nm and 650 nm, respectively, upon excitation at 380 nm. Images of mSiO2 and N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2 under sunlight and a UV lamp are shown in the inset of Fig. S6. The mSiO2 and N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2 are colorless and light brown color under sunlight, respectively, but N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2 shows strong green emission under UV light. In the inset of Fig. S7, BSA-AuNCs appear dark brown under sunlight, but show strong red emission under UV light. N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs in the inset of Fig. 3d show a light brown colour in daylight and emit red light under UV illumination. N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs have dual emission peaks at 520 and 650 nm when excited at 380 nm. The effect of ionic strength on N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs is illustrated in Fig. S8. The addition of NaCl (0.0–0.5 M) does not affect the fluorescence change of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs, indicating that ionic strength does not influence the fluorescence intensity of these nanocomposites. In Fig. S9, we investigated the stability of the N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs by conducting fluorescence measurements over a period of 60 days. The ratio of the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs at 650 nm and 520 nm remained essentially constant, as illustrated, indicating that the N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs exhibit high stability.

The loading capacity of N-CDs and BSA-AuNCs in mSiO2 was studied in Fig. 3e. The mass losses were monitored from 0 ℃-800 ℃. For mSiO2 nanospheres, the initial weight loss below 100 ℃ is due to water evaporation. The weight loss observed above 250 ℃ is mainly due to the decomposition of surface organic groups on the nanospheres. The decomposition of N-CDs and BSA-AuNCs is mainly responsible for the weight loss of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs nanocomposites. The total weight loss of mSiO2 and N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs are 10.18 % and 35.33 %, respectively. Thus, the loading capacity of mSiO2 is approximately 25.15 %. To demonstrate the successful synthesis of the nanocomposites, in Fig. 3f-h we measured the N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms. mSiO2, N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2 and N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs exhibit typical type IV isotherms. These are accompanied by apparent H4-type hysteresis loops, demonstrating that both pre-functionalized and post-functionalized materials are mesoporous. The multi-point brunauer-emmett-teller (BET) surface area and pore volume of mSiO2 are 735.201 m2g−1 and 1.78 cm3g−1, respectively. The BET surface area and pore volume of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2 are 546.521 m2g−1 and 1.61 m3g−1, respectively. For N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs, the BET surface area and pore volume are 365.393 m2g−1 and 1.31 m3g−1, respectively. These results indicate the successful bonding of BSA-AuNCs to the surface of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2.

The material toxicity and biocompatibility of fluorescent nanosensors for N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs were explored by MTT experiments and HeLa cell imaging, respectively. Firstly, MTT experiments were used to test the cytotoxicity of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs. In Fig. S10 as the concentration of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs increased, the viability of HeLa cells decreased. When the concentration was 0.5 mg/mL, the survival rate of cells reached more than 85 %, which proved its low toxicity. Then its cellular imaging was obtained by laser confocal scanning technique. Fig. 4 shows the colocalisation image of Hela cells containing 0.3 mg/mL N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs. It observed in Fig. 4a–d that the cell morphology is well and shuttle-shaped after the N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs fluorescent nanosensor enters the cell. Moreover, all channels (bright, red, green and merge) showed good staining ability, which proved that the fluorescent nanosensor had good biocompatibility.

Fig. S11 displays the UV absorption peaks at 230 nm and 280 nm of BSA-AuNCs in buffer solution (pH 7.40). The peak at 280 nm is attributed to the π → π* B-band absorption of aromatic heterocyclic rings of tryptophan and tyrosine in the BSA molecule, and the n → π* R-band of C=O, which is associated with the ɑ-helical content of the protein's secondary structure. The peak at 230 nm is caused by the π → π* leaps of the aromatic heterocycles [41]. Upon the addition of Co2+, the absorption band at 230 nm in BSA-AuNCs was enhanced and exhibited a slight red shift, while the position of the band at 280 nm remained largely unchanged but showed an increase in intensity. Fig. S12 presents the time-resolved spectra of BSA-AuNCs in the presence and absence of Co2+. The fluorescence lifetime were 1.22 ns and 1.12 ns, respectively. And it is obviously that the change is nearly negligible. This observation suggests the potential for a coordination interaction between BSA-AuNCs and Co2+. These results imply that Co2+ and BSA may form supramolecular complexes through intermolecular forces, thereby altering the α-helical structure of the BSA polypeptide chain. This alteration exposes the aromatic heterocyclic hydrophobic groups of tryptophan and tyrosine residues within the molecule, resulting in a change in the molecular structure of the protein and leading to the quenching of BSA-AuNCs by Co2+.

Fig. 5 a illustrates the fluorescence stability at 520 nm and quenching at 650 nm of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs in the presence of Co2+. The insert in Fig. 5a depicts the plot of F650/F520 against the concentration of Co2+ (2.00–200.00 μM). F650/F520 exhibits a strong linear relationship with Co2+ concentration, with an R value of 0.9978. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the standard deviation of the response (blank) and the slope of the calibration curve. Specifically, the calculation followed the equation:
LOD =
3 σ
S lope
. The LOD was determined to be 0.74 μM, indicating a promising potential for the analysis of Co2+ using the proposed nanosensor. The inset shows the color change yellow to green with increasing Co2+ concentrations under UV light. Table S1 compares various detection methods for Co2+. It is evident from the table that our method exhibits superiority in several aspects. Fig. 5b shows the ratio of fluorescence spectra of N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs when potential interferents (I-, Cl-, S2-, F-, SCN-, NO3 –, CO3 2–, Li+, Na+, K+, Fe3+, Mg2+, NH4 +) are present. The results showed that N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs only responds to Co2+, demonstrating that N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@BSA-AuNCs have good selectivity to Co2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LightGBM training of the fluorescent nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To enhance both the accuracy and speed of Co2+ detection in soil, we developed a real-time prediction model utilizing the LightGBM machine learning algorithm. This model is specifically designed for the rapid and precise prediction of residual Co2+ concentrations based on the RGB values extracted from smartphone images of the fluorescent nanosensor under UV light. The schematic of the LightGBM algorithm for Co2+ concentration detection is illustrated in Fig. 5c. The core principle of the LightGBM algorithm lies in its gradient boosting framework, where each iteration aims to minimize the residual errors from the previous iteration. In this process, the model continually refines its predictions by learning from these residuals, thereby improving accuracy with each subsequent learning cycle. This iterative training process enables LightGBM to effectively capture complex relationships between features and labels, and achieve an outstanding performance among popular machine learning methods. In this paper, the experimental dataset used for model training and testing comprises of RGB values corresponding to three distinct groups of 200 samples, eachexhibiting varying Co2+ concentrations. To prevent any potential bias from unbalanced data values, the RGB values were performed min–max normalization to a range of 0–1 before used as model input. For model training, 80 % of the dataset was divided as the training set, while the remaining 20 % was used as the test set to evaluate the model's performance.

Then we optimized the LightGBM model to enhance its performance in concentration prediction. We set n_estimators as 30, learning_rate of 0.1, and max_depth of 3, which were selected following extensive experimentation and cross-validation. The model was then trained on the training dataset, and its performance was evaluated using R2, MAE, and RMSE. The LightGBM model build in this paper exhibites strong predictive capabilities, achieving a mean R2 value of 0.991 on the test set across three independent datasets which each contains 200 samples. The model's MAE and RMSE are 3.867 and 5.433, respectively, indicating a high degree of accuracy in predicting Co2+ concentrations from RGB values. Conpared to the corresponding researches, the significant advantage of the prediction model in this paper is its high speed. The entire program operates is within 10 s, with RGB value extraction and Co2+ concentration prediction completed under 2 min. Fig. 5d illustrates the rapid detection model that employs smartphone RGB recognition software alongside the LightGBM algorithm. This integration not only expedites the detection process but also improves its applicability in the field, rendering it a valuable tool for environmental monitoring and related applications.

Soil samples collected from various locations within Shanxi University were analyzed for Co2+ multiple times over a period of 10 days using the proposed fluorescent nanosensors in combination with machine learning algorithms. Table 1 presents the results of the actual sample testing, indicating recovery rates ranging from 97.71 % to 102.20 % and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 5 %. To further validate the accuracy of our approach, we employed the LightGBM machine learning algorithm combined with an RGB strategy, achieving recovery rates between 99.82 % and 101.75 %, with an RSD (n = 3) of less than 4 %. These results demonstrate that the LightGBM machine learning algorithm, when integrated with the RGB strategy, provides excellent maneuverability and precision in predicting Co2+ residual concentrations through fluorescence imaging.

In this study, we successfully fabricated and utilized a novel, environmentally friendly, and harmless ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor in conjunction with the LightGBM machine learning algorithm for the quantitative detection of heavy metal Co2+. The fluorescent nanosensor comprises green fluorescent N-CDs encapsulated within mSiO2 to serve as the internal reference signal, while silica spheres are externally bonded with BSA-AuNCs through amidation reactions to function as the fluorescence response signal. Under UV light, the fluorescent color transitions gradually from yellow to green, with different concentrations of Co2+. Additionally, the integration of the LightGBM algorithm significantly enhances both the speed and accuracy of the detection process, ensuring reliability in complex environments. More importantly, this is the first time that a fluorescent nanosensor has been combined with the LightGBM algorithm to detect Co2+.

Chenyue Kang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yueying Li: Writing – original draft, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xuran Wang: Data curation. Yan Zhang: Supervision. Caihong Zhang: Data curation. Ying Zhou: Data curation. Shaomin Shuang: Data curation. Guomei Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (No.202303021221078).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111610.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biphase Stratification Approach to Three-Dimensional Dendritic Biodegradable Mesoporous Silica Nanospheres
Nano Lett
